headlines.title: Paul Pelosi investigated after hit-and-run crash in Yountville
headlines.description: Paul Pelosi, the husband of former House Speaker Nancy Pelosi, is under investigation after deputies say he was involved in a hit-and-run crash in Yountville on Friday afternoon. According to the Napa County Sheriff’s Office, deputies responded around 2:30 p.m. Friday to the 6700 block of Yount Street after a witness reported a hit-and-run crash. The witness told deputies a brown convertible traveling north on Yount Street crashed into an unoccupied, legally parked vehicle, causing major rear damage. The witness said the driver briefly stopped before driving away and provided deputies with the direction the vehicle was traveling. A short time later, deputies found a California Highway Patrol vehicle parked behind a disabled brown convertible that was partially blocking Yountville Cross Road. Deputies identified the driver as Paul Pelosi, 86, through his California driver’s license. The sheriff’s office said the vehicle had significant damage to the front right side that was consistent with the earlier crash on Yount Street. According to investigators, Pelosi admitted he hit something but said he did not know what he had struck and continued driving until his vehicle became disabled and could no longer be driven. Deputies determined Pelosi was the driver involved in the collision. The sheriff’s office said a preliminary alcohol screening test showed a blood alcohol level of 0.00, ruling out driving under the influence. The sheriff’s office said it will submit a California DMV re-evaluation referral, which it described as common for elderly drivers. Pelosi was not arrested, which the sheriff’s office said is common for this type of offense under California misdemeanor arrest laws. The case will be referred to the Napa County District Attorney’s Office for review and possible prosecution.

headlines.title: Power outage affects part of SF's Market Street after equipment damage
headlines.description: About 90 Pacific Gas and Electric customers in San Francisco’s Castro District were still without power on Saturday after a small outage struck a stretch of Market Street on Friday. Traffic signals could be inoperable in the outage area, which included most of the 2200 block of Market Street and the blocks between Noe and Sanchez streets and from 15th to 16th streets, according to the San Francisco Department of Emergency Management. PG&E crews discovered damaged equipment after responding to the outage and were working to restore power by Saturday evening, according to PG&E’s online outage map. The San Francisco Department of Emergency Management reminded drivers in the area to treat inoperable traffic signals as four way stop signs and to use caution.

headlines.title: Joey Chestnut eats 66 hot dogs, wins Mustard Belt at Nathan's Famous contest
headlines.description: Crowds gathered in the sweltering heat Saturday to celebrate the United States’ 250th birthday by watching famed competitive eater Joey “Jaws” Chestnut defend his world hot dog eating championship in Coney Island, where he downed 66 hot dogs in just 10 minutes. Miki Sudo defended her title as well in the women’s division. Saturday marked the 18th time Chestnut, 42, won the so-called Mustard Belt in just 21 appearances at the internationally televised Nathan’s Famous Fourth of July hot dog eating contest. He beat out 13 competitors who came from around the country and world, trekking from the Czech Republic, Australia and South Korea. “It’s a dream, it’s electric, there’s no place better on Earth,” Chestnut said breathlessly in an interview immediately after the showdown, donning a bulky, bejeweled necklace bearing the name of the sports betting company “Polymarket” around his neck. In a video posted on Instagram before the competition, Chestnut called competitive hot dog eating “the most patriotic sport we’ve got.” Chestnut handily defeated the second-place winner, Patrick Bertoletti, 41, who ate 50 hot dogs, but fell short of his own 2021 record of 76 wieners and buns — or approximately 7.6 hot dogs per minute. Joey Chestnut eats hot dogs during the 2025 Nathan’s Famous Fourth of July hot dog eating contest in the Coney Island section of the Brooklyn borough of New York, July 4, 2025. Defending champion in the women’s division, the 40-year-old Sudo of Tampa, Florida, won the bright pink Mustard Belt for the 12th time, downing 38.75 dogs. In 2024, she ate a record 51 links. After competing, Sudo joined the crowd to watch her husband — who proposed to her in 2021 immediately after downing 50 boiled eggs in just over 3 minutes to set a world record — compete in the men’s event. Both champions said that the heatwave that scorched New York on Saturday — bringing temperatures in Brooklyn to roughly 90 degrees Fahrenheit (32 degrees Celsius) during the competition — made that much more difficult. On top of that, ESPN announcers said the humidity changed the consistency of the buns. “I just said I would have to rely on muscle memory, and the crowd really carried me through to another belt today,” Sudo said. A large crowd braved the heat to watch the event, wearing foam hot dogs on their heads and standing outside the original Nathan’s Famous restaurant that has been in Coney Island, Brooklyn, since 1972. The superfans who stood closest to the stage wore ponchos as protection in case a competitor threw up. There was briefly some concern earlier this year that Chestnut wouldn’t be able to participate in the 2026 competition after he was arrested for allegedly slapping a man in an Indiana bar. He was put on probation after pleading guilty to misdemeanor battery, but is allowed to travel outside Indiana. Hot dog contest organizer Major League Eating said the criminal case didn’t affect Chestnut’s eligibility for the competition. Despite his continued reign, Chestnut told an ESPN interviewer after the game that he was disappointed that he wasn’t able to top his previous record. “Dang, I got so much room left,” said Chestnut, who announcers referred to as the “Michelangelo of Mastication.”

headlines.title: Vegetation fire burns Adobe Loop Trail area in Mountain View
headlines.description: Firefighters were battling a fire burning both sides of Adobe Loop Trail in Mountain View. The Mountain View Fire Department said the fire was burning a quarter of an acre on both sides of the Adobe Loop Trail. The forward progress of the fire was stopped, according to fire authorities.

headlines.title: Bay Area 4th of July events guide 2026
headlines.description: Hello, Summer! It’s time for our yearly roundup of Fourth of July events taking place around the San Francisco Bay Area. Below is what we’ve compiled so far, listed in alphabetical order by city. If you have others you’d like to see on this list, tweet us at @nbcbayarea your suggestions. 🎉= Event will also celebrate America250 🎉Alameda: 4th of July aboard the USS Hornet 10 a.m. to 5 p.m. Enjoy BBQ food, drinks and music on the flight deck. The ship’s island will be open for free tours. This year come celebrate “250 Years of the Hornet Legacy.” uss-hornet.org Alameda: 4th of July run 8:30 a.m. to 11 a.m. A 5K run through Alameda starting just before the city’s 4th of July parade. The run benefits the Midway Shelter for Women and children. runsignup.com 🎉Alameda: 4th of July parade 10 a.m. Attendees should arrive early to cheer on the runners in the Alameda 5K run preceding the parade. Celebration of America 250 and 150 years of the Alameda Fire Department. alamedaca.gov Albany: 4th of July in the Park 11 a.m. to 3 p.m. Hosted at Memorial Park, Albany’s 4th of July event will have food, live bands and more. There will be an ice cream giveaway at 1 p.m. albanyca.org American Canyon: 4th of July Celebration Parade starts at 3 p.m. Festival starts at 4 p.m. Fireworks will begin at dusk. Check out an afternoon parade along Elliott Drive followed by a festival with music, food and free activities. americancanyon.gov 🎉Antioch: Fourth of July Celebration Parade starts at 4 p.m. and the fireworks show will begin around 9:20 p.m. Hosted at Waldie Plaza, Antioch’s 4th of July event will have food, live bands, a family zone and more. antiochca.gov. Concord: 4th of July Concord festivities 8 a.m. – Stars and Stripes kids fun run at Todos Santos Plaza. 10 a.m. – Independence Day parade starts at Todos Santos Plaza. 4 p.m. – Festival gates open at Mt. Diablo High School. 9 p.m. – Fireworks start at Mt. Diablo High School. concordjuly4th.com Cupertino: 4th of July Celebration 8 a.m. – Pancake breakfast at Quinlan Community Center. 10 a.m. – Flag raising ceremony and Children’s parade at Memorial Park. 10:30 a.m. – Concert and kids activities at Memorial Park. 9:30 p.m. – Fireworks start at Hyde Middle School, check the website for viewing locations. cupertino.gov Danville: Kiwanis-Danville 4th of July parade 9 a.m. to 12 p.m. Along Hartz Avenue and San Ramon Valley Boulevard. srvkiwanis.org/parade El Cerrito: 4th of July Festival 11 a.m. to 5 p.m. Co-hosted by the city and worldOne, the festival itself is free but tickets can be purchase for inflatables or rides. elcerrito.org 🎉Fairfield: Independence Day Parade Starts at 10 a.m. The theme for this years’ parade is America 250. Parade starts at Webster and Kentucky Street. Festival floats, marching bands, classic cars, dancers and community groups. fairfield.ca.gov Foster City: Fourth of July celebration 8:30 a.m. to 4 p.m. Games, food, entertainment, a dog and family parade all happen at Leo J. Ryan Park. There will be no firework show this year. fostercity.org Gilroy: Fireworks Show The show starts at 9:30 p.m. Located at Gilroy High School. Guests are encouraged to walk or bike to the show to avoid traffic. cityofgilroy.org/fireworks Half Moon Bay: Ol’ Fashioned 4th of July Parade Pancake breakfast at City Hall from 8 a.m. to 11:30 p.m. Parade starts at 12 p.m. on Main Street IDES 4th of July Festival from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. featuring live music, food, a kids corner and more. hmbfourthofjuly.com Healdsburg: 4th of July Kids’ Parade and Duck Dash 10 a.m. to 1 p.m. Live music, lawn games, Duck Dash Races, face painting and a parade around the plaza. Event takes place in downtown Healdsburg Plaza. healdsburg.gov Healdsburg: Independence Day fireworks Fireworks start at 9:30 p.m. You can view the fireworks at select areas outside of Healdsburg High School and Fitch Mountain Elementary School. healdsburg.gov Los Altos Hills: 4th of July Parade 9 a.m. to 11 a.m. Gather at town hall prior to the parade. Parade will start at Fremont Road and conclude at Gardner Bullis School. losalthoshills.ca.gov Los Gatos: 4th of July Symphony in the Park 10 a.m. to 1 p.m. Activities and food are also available at Oak Meadow Park. Schedule of events coming soon. losgatosca.gov Martinez: Downtown Martinez 4th of July Parade Starts at 10 a.m. Come early to watch the flag raising ceremony at the Veteran’s Memorial building before the parade. downtownmartinez.org Menlo Park: 4th of July community parade, picnic and circus 11 a.m. to 3 p.m. Bring a picnic basket and blanket, dress in red, white, and blue, to celebrate with fun events. Meet for the parade outside the Arrillaga Family Gymnasium parking lot. Other events include a community picnic, circus performance and food trucks. menlopark.gov Milpitas: 4th of July – Red, White and Boom! Fireworks and Concert 5 p.m. to 9 p.m. Located at Milpitas Sports Center. Events include a DJ, bounce house, carnival games and much more. Tickets are available for purchase online or in-person. milpitas.gov/4thofJuly Moraga: 4th of July Celebration 11 a.m. dog parade. Live music from 12 p.m. to 3 p.m. and at 7 p.m. Afternoon family activities, community booths, food and drink, and inflatable jumpies. 9:30 p.m. estimated fireworks at Moraga Commons Park. moraga.ca.us 🎉Morgan Hill: Freedom Fest Events throughout the day and fireworks at night. No more entry into fireworks venue after 9 p.m. This year’s theme is “America: Celebrating 250 Years of Dreamers, Doers and Believers.” Events include Patriotic Sing, Family Music Fest, Freedom Run, Parade, Cruise n’ Car Show, annual essay contest and a Fireworks on the Green. morganhillfreedomfest.com Mountain View: Fourth of July Fireworks Spectacular Concert starts at 7 p.m. and fireworks shortly after. Enjoy a concert from the San Francisco Symphony at the Shoreline Amphitheater. You can also view the fireworks outside for free at Shoreline Park. mountainview.gov 🎉Napa: 4th of July parade 9:30 a.m. parade along 2nd and 3rd streets. Activities, vendors, performances, a drone show and more throughout the day. napa4thofjulyparade.com Pacifica: Fourth of July Celebration and Rocket Run 11 a.m. to 2 p.m. Located at Frontierland Park Enjoy food and drinks, games, rides, live music, and more. Rocket Run check-in begins at 8 a.m. and the run will start at 9 a.m. The first 75 people to arrive will receive a t-shirt. cityofpacifica.org Palo Alto: 4th of July Summer Festival and Chili Cook-Off 11 a.m. to 3 p.m. Located in Mitchell Park, people can come and enjoy food trucks, live music and — of course — the chili cook-off. cityofpaloalto.org 🎉Pittsburg: July 4th Fireworks Show 9 p.m. to 11 p.m. Located at the Pittsburg Marina. The event includes fireworks, food trucks, and a Waterfront Firework Party. Join in for a celebration of America 250. Space is limited so arrive early. pittsburgca.gov Pleasant Hill: Celebrating the 4th of July Events throughout the day; 9:30 a.m. parade, 8:45 p.m. fireworks show. The viewing party for fireworks returns to College Park and fields open at 6:30 p.m. $4 per person suggested donation. phjuly4.com 🎉Redwood City: Family 4th of July Parade Pancake breakfast at 8 a.m. Parade at 10 a.m., then a festival showcasing local organizations and artists. Drone show at 9:30 p.m. over the Port of Redwood City. This year’s theme is “America 250.” parade.org 🎉San Francisco: 7X7 Light Installation June 21 to July 4 at Civic Center Plaza Free public lights installation with a USA theme on July 4 sf.gov/7×7 🎉San Francisco: America 250 Picnic and Speeches 12 p.m. to 4 p.m. Located at Fort Mason Park. Opportunity to read a short, historic speech. Live music to accompany. Fireworks will be shown nearby in the evening. america250.org/san-francisco San Francisco: Fourth of July fireworks on Golden Gate Bridge Fireworks show will begin at 9:30 p.m. Recommended viewing locations include Crissy Field, Marina Green, Pier 39 and the northern Embarcadero. Attendees are advised to arrive early due to traffic and limited parking. sf.gov/july4 🎉San Jose: 4th of July Celebration at Lake Cunningham Festival starts at 6 p.m., Drone Show starts at 9 p.m. Located at Lake Cunningham Park. Food, live entertainment and activities for kids. sanjoseca.gov/4thofJuly San Jose: Rose, White and Blue parade Kickoff – 9:45 a.m. Parade – 10 a.m. to 12 p.m. next to Lincoln High School. Festival – 11 a.m. to 2 p.m. Parade, entertainment, food and more. Historic celebration of diversity, public spirit and the community’s floral/agricultural heritage. rwbsj.org 🎉San Leandro: 250th Independence Day Event 11 a.m. to 4 p.m. at Monarch Bay Golf Course There will be live music, artisan vendors, food trucks and activities for all ages. No fireworks will be included. sanleandro.org San Pablo: 4th of July Multi-Cultural Celebration Doors open at 5:30 p.m. Drone show starts at 9:30 p.m. Enjoy live entertainment, carnival games, food, resource tables and a drone show. sanpabloca.gov 🎉Santa Clara: 4th of July Community Celebration 5 p.m. to 10 p.m. Located at Mission College. Live entertainment, food and fun activities will be available. Fireworks will begin at 9:40 p.m. and can be viewed at Central Plaza or Parking Lot A. santaclaraca.gov/4thofJuly Sausalito: 4th of July festivities 10 a.m. parade starts in Old Town; 12 p.m. to 4 p.m. picnic at Dunphy Park. 9:15 p.m. fireworks show at Gabrielson Park. Enjoy live music from Top Shelf Classics while waiting for the fireworks. sausalito.gov 🎉South San Francisco: 4th of July Parade and Picnic 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. Parade starts at Orange Memorial Park and moves to City Hall. Celebrate America 250 with family-friendly activities, entertainment, giveaways and more. ssfca.gov 🎉Sunnyvale: 4th of July Festival 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. Located in Downtown Sunnyvale. Enjoy amusement rides, carnival games, slides, performances and more for America 250. There will be a bicycle parade at 9:30 a.m. sunnyvale.ca.gov 🎉Vallejo: Mare Island Spirit Ship Celebration 5:30 p.m. to 9:30 p.m. Featuring a 125 drone show by Sky Elements above the Mare Island waterfront. Includes food trucks, beverages, treats and kids’ games and activities. It is recommended to bring your own lawn chairs or blankets. america250.org/mare-island Vallejo: Star-Spangled Nights at Six Flags Discovery Kingdom 9:15 p.m. on Friday and Saturday. Watch the drone show from rides and spots across the theme park. sixflags.com

headlines.title: San Francisco Fourth of July: officials say to plan for traffic and avoid cliffs
headlines.description: San Francisco is slated for a historic Fourth of July celebration on Wednesday, with fireworks set to be lit from the towers of the Golden Gate Bridge for only the third time ever. This celebration is set to mark America’s 250th anniversary. Public officials are warning people headed to watch the fireworks that especially large crowds and especially big traffic delays are expected this year. The Golden Gate Bridge will be completely closed from 9 to 10 p.m., with lane closures starting at 8 p.m., CalTrans said. San Francisco Fire Department Rescue Captain Samuel Menchaca said firefighters are urging the public not to attempt to watch the fireworks displays from cliffsides. He explained that many cliffs in San Francisco are steeper and more unstable than they appear. “We want you to stay on the trails, don’t go off the trails, and if someone falls or goes down the cliff, to not go and get them and to call 911,” Menchaca said. Instead, he said the best places to watch the fireworks from San Francisco will be Crissy Field, Marina Green, Pier 39, and Fisherman’s Wharf. Menchaca noted there will be plenty of space for people to watch between these locations, but that people will need to plan ahead and get there early to avoid traffic. “Transit and parking over there is going to be difficult; we encourage you to get dropped off or use public transit to get over there safely,” he said. Pedicab drivers in the area said they are hoping the stream of visitors will bring more profits. Driver Brian Giesler said that typically on Fourth of July, crowds tend to get very big starting at around 6 p.m. “It’s horrible in the financial district, after the fireworks, every street is completely blocked,” he noted.

headlines.title: East Bay teen with Stage 4 cancer shares journey on social media to inspire others
headlines.description: For many young people, social media is a place to share everyday moments. For 19-year-old Elijah Dawkins of San Ramon, it has become a journal of resilience after he was diagnosed with Stage 4 cancer. Dawkins was diagnosed with Stage 4 Hodgkin lymphoma on his 19th birthday after finding an enlarged lymph node under his arm – explaining years of pain he thought came from high school sports. “It was heartbreaking, it really was,” he said. “I just thank God because they told me, first of all, it’s curable. It did spread literally kind of throughout my whole right side of my body, but honestly, any 19-year-old would be very crushed.” His first video reached more than 1 million views. Now, every post documents the highs and lows of treatment. “I really feel like talking about your things and not holding it in, it relieves stress,” he said. “And even just talking about God, I talk to God all the time about it.” Dawkins credits his mom for teaching him to never give up. Now on day 109 of his six-month treatment plan, he’s using every post as a way to process while inspiring others. With more than 75,000 followers on Instagram alone, he’s created a community that helps him know he is not fighting alone. “That is one of the biggest things that’s helping me get through this process to be honest,” he said. “Seeing the support, that literally helps me go every single day and just share my journey.” Dawkins is still going to college and working, saying cancer has taught him not to waste time chasing his goals. He hopes others learn that lesson, too. “There’s a lot of people who go through the same things, and it’s really important to not fall a victim of things and just keep pushing because life happens, things happen,” he said. Next week, Dawkins will learn if his treatment is working. He’s staying focused on his future dreams of owning his own business and growing his platform. “People are going to watch me grow, beat this and be very successful later on in life,” he said. “This is really just the beginning, and this is the battle that I’m fighting every single day. After this, it’s going to be a lot easier.”

headlines.title: Santa Clara County authorities prepare por possible use of illegal fireworks on July Fourth
headlines.description: The use of illegal fireworks is a Fourth of July activity that police and fire agencies have to watch out for starting Friday night. A little spot near an overpass in San Jose has been a hot spot for illegal fireworks before, and some people who live nearby say neighborhood shows are scary because they often take place near homes. A homeowners’ group in San Jose has videotaped a lot of illegal fireworks activity over the years. Jeff Levine of the Roosevelt Park neighborhood Association says increased enforcement by police and fire agencies has helped, but illegal fireworks are still an ongoing concern. “Every night, every night there’s a handful. Some of them are quite close, and there are aerial explosives. Yeah, you still have them, but it’s significantly less than in years past,” he said. Santa Clara County’s fire agency says it is much more aggressive about enforcement. They plan extra patrols that coordinate with police. San Jose encourages the public to use a city app to report illegal fireworks and say they will also be using ‘drones’ to zero in on activity. Cal Fire is also on increased alert, especially since stepping up enforcement in the cities could send people into the dry hills to set off their fireworks. “Cal Fire’s approach will be to staff additional prevention officers and our firefighters are ready for the any challenges that may come, but we’re hoping everybody stays safe. We’re urging the community to leave the fireworks to the professionals and if you are in a jurisdiction that does allow fireworks make sure it’s the safe and sane fireworks.” Cal Fire Unit Chief Marcus Hernandez said. The only Santa Clara County city that allows the sale of safe and sane fireworks is in Gilroy, where sellers, such as Victory Outreach, hand out copies of city regulations and warnings along with the fireworks. “We do ask for a Gilroy ID making sure that they are residents and then, for safety, give them the city ordinance information, letting them know these are the areas that they are able to perform their fireworks, but there is a clear sheet with clear instructions on that information that we hand them and give them,” Victory Outreach worker Elisa Jimenez said. Along with fines and other penalties for possessing or setting off illegal fireworks, officials also point out even those who knowingly attend and watch an illegal fireworks display can be cited.

headlines.title: Mexico-England World Cup watch party moved from San Pedro Square
headlines.description: San Jose is moving its official World Cup watch parties to SAP Center and Discovery Meadow for Sunday’s match between Mexico and England. San Pedro Square has drawn tens of thousands of fans for World Cup matches so far, with crowds regularly filling the area. City officials expect even more fans for Sunday’s match and said the larger venues are needed to accommodate the anticipated turnout. “We’re showing the rest of the country what it means to be a soccer city,” Mayor Matt Mahan said in a statement. “The enthusiasm and energy our community is bringing to these matches is incredible. On the eve of historic matchups between Mexico and England, and Brazil and Norway, we’re opening larger venues where fans and families can come together and celebrate safely.” During Tuesday’s match featuring Mexico and Ecuador, more than 40,000 fans tried to crowd into San Pedro Square. Many were turned away. Following the match and watch party, two people were stabbed and several others were arrested. Discovery Meadow will show both the Mexico-England (5 p.m.) and Brazil-Norway (1 p.m.) matches, while SAP Center will show Mexico-England. Tickets for both watch parties sold out quickly Friday. While the official watch parties will be held at SAP Center and Discovery Meadow, the bars and restaurants in San Pedro Square will remain open Sunday. The official watch parties will return to San Pedro Square on Monday for Team USA’s Round of 16 match against Belgium. Some San Pedro Square business owners said they have mixed feelings about the change. “I will miss the atmosphere,” O’Flaherty’s Irish Pub owner David Mulvehill said. “I’m going to miss the atmosphere a lot. Those games were a lot of fun. But I get where the city is coming from. You got too many people. Sometimes safety is a priority.” San Jose Jul 1 World Cup Jun 30 San Jose erupts as Mexico wins first World Cup knockout match in 40 years

headlines.title: Fourth of July cookout will cost more this year. Here's why
headlines.description: Those who are about to host or attend a Fourth of July barbeque will have to be prepared to pay a little more this year. The cost of meat has risen steadily this year; higher farming costs and some scarcity means beef costs 5.5% more this July Fourth than last. Chicken and pork are also higher. Higher agriculture costs mean strawberries cost more. Scott Budman has the full report in the video above.

headlines.title: Petaluma cookbook author creates signature drink for all 50 states
headlines.description: A cookbook author in Petaluma has created a new signature drink for all 50 states, in honor of America’s 250th birthday. The recipes for every single one are in a new cocktail cookbook called “Spirits Of ’76.” It was put together by Kim Laidlaw, a Bay Area trained baker. In addition to the new signature drink, she also picked a classic cocktail for each state. NBC Bay Area’s Janelle Wang spoke to the author, who said she spent months researching and taste testing to pull it all together. Watch the full interview in the video above.

headlines.title: BART reports fare evasion down in its 50 stations. Viral video might show why
headlines.description: BART said fare evasion is on the decline at its 50 stations, and a new viral video appears to show one reason why. A viral video circulating on social media shows a woman stuck under BART’s new fare gates, presumably caught while trying to slip through without paying. “People do some of the craziest things at BART. It was really not a smart thing to do they try to crawl under a fare gate,” Former BART Director Debora Allen said. Former BART Director Debora Allen helped spearhead the project. She says the 6-foot gates framed with aluminum were carefully designed to deter fare evaders and she says she’s delighted to see them working. “I think it’s great. I think it’s the best thing we’ve done at BART in many, probably decades. When place 1970 era would I call pong era fair gates, replacing them with new technology,” Allen said. “We’re really happy that we’re seeing the results of this $90 million project that’s going to be paid off potentially in 10 years or less,” current BART Director Liz Ames said. Ames says as fare evasion has plummeted BART revenue has gone up. She says revenue has grown by $10 million annually thanks to the new fare gates. She says BART is also saving money on maintenance and cleaning. Less fare evaders have meant less litter and graffiti, and she says, less crime. “Overall crime is dropping and we think we can that can be attributed to the fair gates in some sense,” Ames said. While the gates have helped, they aren’t full proof. NBC Bay Area saw a woman trying to slip through when another passenger opened the gate. While she didn’t succeed the first time, we witnessed her piggybacking her way in when our cameras weren’t rolling. “I would say it happens less, but it still happens,” Ames said. But the video shows the risk riders take if they try to avoid the fee. “I hope she wasn’t hurt and I hope she received a nice ticket to pay for fare evasion,” Allen said.
